source.title: The effect of child-appropriate play with various types on preschoolers' executive function

confidence_rationale: RCT with 3-month follow-up directly comparing EF outcomes across play types (movement, puzzle, language play). High source quality for design. Mapping fidelity is moderate: the study uses play categories that do not directly map to Piaget/Smilansky play types, but the finding that rule-based structures within play drive EF gains -- regardless of specific play type -- directly evidences MECH-197's mechanism claim. Transfer risk is low.
failure_signatures: Working memory effects of movement play were not maintained at 3-month follow-up -- play-type specificity for WM is weak.
Play categories (movement, puzzle, language) do not directly map to the sensorimotor/constructive/pretend/rule-games taxonomy, limiting direct MECH-197 mapping.
No active control matched for adult engagement -- facilitator effects cannot be fully ruled out.

mapping.source_claim_statement: All three play types (movement, puzzle, language) improved inhibitory control and cognitive flexibility; working memory effects varied by type. The critical shared mechanism was adult-guided, rule-based structure integrated systematically into play activities, in synergy with emotional regulation demands.
mapping.ree_translation: The finding that rule-based structure (not play type per se) drives EF gains aligns with MECH-197: the structured constraint system within the play frame trains the specific EF capacities (inhibitory control = impulse suppression for rule adherence; cognitive flexibility = switching between rule-constrained roles/turns). This confirms that games-with-rules train constraint satisfaction as a competence, supporting DEV-NEED-013.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The play interventions are adult-scaffolded and directed, not peer-generated. The social coordination and bilateral frame-maintenance dimensions of games-with-rules (relevant to ARC-049/INV-059) are not the target -- this study tests EF training, not shared-state monitoring development. Cannot speak to Q-035.
mapping.source_context: 4-5 year old children, 10-week RCT with 3-month follow-up, N=62, China.

confidence_components.notes: RCT design with follow-up is strong; play categories are non-standard which reduces mapping fidelity.
